elements.sent-2411-001: In this paper, we consider the problem of finding weak independent sets in a distributed network represented by a hypergraph.
elements.sent-2411-002: In this setting, each edge contains a set of $r$ vertices rather than simply a pair, as in a standard graph.
elements.sent-2411-003: A $k$-weak independent set in a hypergraph is a set where no edge contains more than $k$ vertices in the independent set.
elements.sent-2411-004: We focus two variations of this problem.
elements.sent-2411-005: First, we study the problem of finding $k$-weak maximal independent sets, $k$-weak independent sets where each vertex belongs to at least one edge with $k$ vertices in the independent set.
elements.sent-2411-006: Second we introduce a weaker variant that we call $(\alpha, \beta)$-independent sets where the independent set is $\beta$-weak, and each vertex belongs to at least one edge with at least $\alpha$ vertices in the independent set.
elements.sent-2411-007: Finally, we consider the problem of finding a $(2, k)$-ruling set on hypergraphs, i.e. independent sets where no vertex is a distance of more than $k$ from the nearest member of the set.
elements.sent-2411-008: Given a hypergraph $H$ of rank $r$ and maximum degree $\Delta$, we provide a LLL formulation for finding an $(\alpha, \beta)$-independent set when $(\beta - \alpha)^2 / (\beta + \alpha) \geq 6 \log(16 r \Delta)$, an $O(\Delta r / (\beta - \alpha + 1) + \log^* n)$ round deterministic algorithm finding an $(\alpha, \beta)$-independent set, and a $O(\Delta^2(r - k) \log r + \Delta \log r \log^* r + \log^* n)$ round algorithm for finding a $k$-weak maximal independent set.
elements.sent-2411-009: Additionally, we provide zero round randomized algorithms for finding $(\alpha, \beta)$ independent sets, when $(\beta - \alpha)^2 / (\beta + \alpha) \geq 6 c \log n + 6$ for some constant $c$, and finding an $m$-weak independent set for some $m \geq r / 2k$ where $k$ is a given parameter.
elements.sent-2411-010: Finally, we provide lower bounds of $\Omega(\Delta + \log^* n)$ and $\Omega(r + \log^* n)$ on the problems of finding a $k$-weak maximal independent sets for some values of $k$.
elements.sent-2411-011: Independent sets are a key tool in distributed networks, allowing nodes within a graph to assert themselves as leaders over their immediate neighbors so as to help with the allocation of resources.
elements.sent-2411-012: This is of particular importance in the distributed setting, where nodes likely do not have full information of the complete graph.
elements.sent-2411-013: The best known version of the independent set problem is the maximal independent set (MIS) problem.
elements.sent-2411-014: Informally, in non-hypergraphs, an independent set is a subset of vertices such that no pair of vertices belong to an edge in the graph.
elements.sent-2411-015: Such a set is maximal if every vertex either belongs to to the set, or has some neighbor in the set.
elements.sent-2411-016: As this property is locally checkable \cite{linial1992locality , each vertex in the graph can determine whether it satisfies this condition by considering only the local neighborhood.}
elements.sent-2411-017: In other words, once an algorithm to find an MIS has terminated, each vertex can determine if the algorithm was locally successful by checking if it belongs to the independent set, and if any of its neighbors do.\looseness=-1 Due to the fundament nature of the problem, finding independent sets in distributed graphs is one of the most heavily studied problems in distributed computing.
elements.sent-2411-018: Starting with the classic algorithm due to Linial \cite{linial1987distributive,linial1992locality , there has been a successive series of improvements in terms of both deterministic \cite{awerbuch1989network,barenboim2016locality,panconesi2001some,barenboim2009distributed and randomized \cite{kutten2014distributed,kuhn2018efficient,ghaffari2016improved,ghaffari2021improved algorithms.}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: At the same time, there has been a significant body of work determining the lower bound on the number of rounds required for distributed algorithms in general \cite{brandt2016lower,brandt2019automatic , and finding a MIS in particular \cite{balliu2022distributed,balliu2021improved,balliu2021lower .}}
elements.sent-2411-020: As it stands, the current state-of-the-art for solving MIS deterministically, in terms of the maximum degree of the graph, matches the lower bound, giving an $O(\Delta + \log^* n)$ round algorithm for finding an MIS in an $n$ node graph, with a maximum degree of $\Delta$.\looseness=-1 There are various ways of generalizing the MIS problem to the hypergraph setting.
elements.sent-2411-021: The best studied is the weak-MIS problem.
elements.sent-2411-022: A set $S$ is a weak-MIS if no hyperedge in the graph is a subset of $S$.
elements.sent-2411-023: This is the primary version studied in the distributed setting, with both positive, algorithmic results \cite{kutten2014distributed and lower bounds \cite{Balliu2023-distributed .}}
elements.sent-2411-024: In this paper, we consider two generalization of the independent set problem to hypergraphs, $k$-weak independent sets, and $(\alpha, \beta)$-independent sets.
elements.sent-2411-025: A $k$-weak independent set is a set $S$ such that the intersection between any edge in the graph and $S$ has size at most $k$.
elements.sent-2411-026: Such a set is maximal if every vertex in the hypergraph either belongs to $S$, or belongs to some edge containing $k$ other vertices in $S$.
elements.sent-2411-027: An $(\alpha, \beta)$-independent set is a $\beta$-weak independent set $S$, where each vertex is either in $S$, or belongs to at least one edge containing $\alpha$ vertices in $S$.
elements.sent-2411-028: Note that a $(k, k)$-independent set is thus a $k$-weak MIS.
elements.sent-2411-029: We note that both definitions are locally checkable, in the same manner as above.\looseness=-1 Of particular interest to our paper are the works of Kutten et al. \cite{kutten2014distributed , Kuhn and Zheng \cite{kuhn2018efficient , and Balliu et al. \cite{Balliu2023-distributed , all of which analyze the MIS problem on hypergraphs.}}}

elements.sent-2411-032: Once partitioned, each component may ``centralize'' the topology of the local neighborhood into a single node, that can then solve the problem, broadcasting the solution to the other nodes in its component.

elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{Algorithm for finding a $(2, k)$-Ruling Set in the hypergraph $H$.} \label{alg:FindRS} \begin{algorithmic} \Procedure{FindRS}{Hypergraph $H$, $k \in \mathbb{N}$} \State $InIS \gets True$ \% Initially, we say every vertex is in the independent set. \State $I \gets V$ \% Add every vertex to the independent set. \State $r' = r$ \% Current \While{$InIS$ and $r' > 2$} \State $E' \gets \{e \cap I \mid e \in E \}$ \% Update the edges with the neighbors of $v$ still in the independent set. \State EdgePartitionIS($(I, E')$, $(2 r' / 3, r' / 3)$) \State $r' \gets 2 r' / 3$ \If{$v$ not in the independent set} \State $InIS \gets False$ \EndIf \State Update independent set $I$. \EndWhile \If{$InIS$} \State $E' \gets \{e \cap I \mid e \in E \}$ \% Update the edges with the neighbors of $v$ still in the independent set. \State FindIS$($Underlying Graph $(I, E')$ $)$ \% Using known algorithms, this wil take $O(r' \Delta + \log^* n) = O(\Delta + \log^* n)$ rounds. \EndIf \EndProcedure \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-297: In what follows below, we describe a slight modification of this procedure that computes a $k$-MIS.
elements.sent-2411-298: Before describing the procedure more formally, we introduce some notation.
elements.sent-2411-299: Suppose $S$ is an independent set in $H = (V, E)$ and $k$ is an integer with $1 \leq k \leq r - 1$.
elements.sent-2411-300: The saturation of an edge $e$ is $\sat(e) = \abs{e \cap S}$.
elements.sent-2411-301: We say $e$ is saturated if $\sat(e) = k$.
elements.sent-2411-302: We say that $v$ is active if (1) $v \notin S$, and (2) $v$ is not contained in a saturated edge.
elements.sent-2411-303: We denote the set of active vertices in $e$ by $A_e$ and say that $e$ is inactive if $A_e = \varnothing$.
elements.sent-2411-304: We say that $e$ is in phase $\varphi$ if $(1 - 2^{-\varphi - 1 )k < \sat(e) \leq (1 - 2^{-\varphi )k$.}}
elements.sent-2411-305: We denote the phase of $e$ by $\varphi(e)$.
elements.sent-2411-306: We make the following observation about the phase of edges.
elements.sent-2411-307: If a edge $e$ is in phase $\varphi = \log r + 1$, then $e$ is saturated.
elements.sent-2411-308: In particular, each edge's phase can increase at most $\log r$ times before the edge is either saturated or inactive.
elements.sent-2411-309: The main procedure assumes that the vertices in the input are properly colored (in the underlying graph) with $O(\Delta^2 r^2)$ colors.
elements.sent-2411-310: Such a coloring can be found in $O(\log^* n)$ rounds using the algorithm of Linial~\cite{linial1992locality .}
elements.algorithm-2411-004: \caption{$k$-weak MIS\label{alg:k-weak-mis}} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Procedure{kWeakMIS}{$H$} \State $S \gets \varnothing$\Comment{The IS} \State for each edge $e$, $A_e \gets e$\Comment{Active vertices in $e$} \For{each iteration $i = 1, 2, \ldots, 1 + \Delta \log r$}\label{ln:lp-start} \If{$\abs{A_e} \geq 4 (r - k)$}\label{ln:partition-start} \State partition $A_e$ into $\lfloor \abs{A_e}/4 \rfloor$ parts of size at most $5$ \Else \State keep $A_e$ as a single part \EndIf\label{ln:partition-end} \State $H' \gets$ the hypergraph induced by this edge partition \State properly color $H'$ with $4 \Delta (r - k) + 1$ colors\label{ln:color} \For{each color class $c$} \State $V_c \gets$ vertices of color $c$ \If{$v \in V_c$ and each $e \ni v$ satisfies $\abs{V_c \cap e} \leq k - \sat(e)$} \State $v$ adds itself to $S$ and removes itself from $A_e$\label{ln:add-self} \EndIf \EndFor \If{$v$ is is incident to a saturated edge} \State remove $v$ from all incident $A_e$ \EndIf \EndFor\label{ln:lp-end} \EndProcedure \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-311: We now state and prove our main result for this section.
elements.sent-2411-312: Let $H = (V, E)$ be an $r$-uniform hypergraph with maximum degree at most $\Delta$.
elements.sent-2411-313: Then on input $H$, Algorithm~\ref{alg:k-weak-mis produces a $k$-weak MIS in $O(\Delta^2 (r - k) \log r + \Delta \log r \log^* r + \log^* n)$ rounds in the LOCAL model.}

elements.sent-2411-331: In this paper, we have provided an overview of the $k$-MIS and $(\alpha, \beta)$-IS problems.
elements.sent-2411-332: Explicitly, we have shown lower bounds in terms of both $r$ and $\Delta$ on finding a $1$-MIS, and in terms of $\Delta$ on finding a $k$-MIS for certain values of $k$.
elements.sent-2411-333: In the other direction, we have provided a set of algorithmic results for finding both $k$-weak independent sets, and $(\alpha, \beta)$ independent sets.
elements.sent-2411-334: Our lower bound results suggest that finding $k$-weak MIS may be computationally costly, but the weaker $(\alpha, \beta)$-IS readily admits efficient algorithms for some range of parameters.
elements.sent-2411-335: Thus, in applications where some form of maximal independent set is desirable, the an $(\alpha, \beta)$-IS may offer sufficient ``maximality'' while allowing for significantly faster algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-336: While our results provide some bounds on the LOCAL complexity of computing $k$-weak MIS and $(\alpha, \beta)$-IS, there are still significant gaps between the upper and lower bounds.
elements.sent-2411-337: Even for the case of $1$-weak MIS our lower bound $\Omega(\Delta + r + \log^* n)$ is quite far from the upper bound of $O(r \Delta + \log^* n)$.
elements.sent-2411-338: What is the LOCAL complexity of finding $1$-weak MIS? Our algorithms show that for $k = r - o(r)$, the trivial upper bound of $r \Delta + \log^* n$ is not tight, at least for $\Delta$ much smaller than $r$.
elements.sent-2411-339: Can such improvements be found for $k = r - \Omega(r)$? More generally, what is the relationship between the complexities of $k$-weak MIS and $k'$-weak MIS? To this end, we observe that for $k < k'$, a $k$-weak MIS can always be extended to a $k'$-weak MIS.
elements.sent-2411-340: On the other hand, a $k'$-weak MIS need not contain any $k$-weak MIS.
elements.sent-2411-341: This seems to suggest that perhaps finding $k$-weak MIS becomes harder for smaller values of $k$.
elements.sent-2411-342: Indeed, we were only able to prove our $\Omega(\Delta)$ lower bound for $k = 1$.
elements.sent-2411-343: In general, finding $(\alpha, \beta)$-IS seems much easier than finding $k$-weak MIS when $\beta - \alpha$ is reasonably large.
elements.sent-2411-344: Indeed, for $\alpha = 1$ and $\beta \sim c \log (r \Delta)$, $(\alpha, \beta)$-IS can be found without any communication.
elements.sent-2411-345: More generally, how does the complexity of finding an $(\alpha, \beta)$-IS vary with $\Delta$, $r$, and $\delta = \beta - \alpha + 1$? Our algorithms in Section~\ref{sec:deterministic-algorithms relied upon finding $\delta$-edge defective colorings in hypergraphs---i.e., colorings in which each color class's intersection with each edge is at most $\delta$.}
elements.sent-2411-346: Our approach employed known algorithms for (proper) graph coloring to find $\delta$-edge defective colorings.
elements.sent-2411-347: We think it would be interesting perform a more thorough investigation of distributed edge-defective colorings in hypergraphs.
